authored.title: Luxury Shopify hamper line — 13-hamper editorial retouch: per-frame geometry + masked tonal grade, selectively-enhanced embossed leather badge, warmed wicker, two-stage depth blur, shared quiet-luxury preset, cut-out onto a light-grey studio backdrop with a realistic contact shadow, Shopify exports
authored.one_line_ask: Edit 13 luxury gifting-hamper photos to one quiet-luxury (Fortnum & Mason / Daylesford) look — geometry-correct each frame, build a masked tonal grade (warm wicker, opened shadows, restrained colour), selectively enhance the embossed leather front badge, add two-stage editorial depth blur, lock a single shared Lightroom preset across the set, then cut every hamper out onto a clean light-grey (#E8E8E8) website backdrop with a realistic contact shadow and export Shopify-ready 2048×2048 squares.
deliverables: 13 high-resolution edited JPGs sharing ONE quiet-luxury recipe (matched exposure, white balance, opened shadows, restrained saturation, locked final preset)
Every hamper cut out cleanly and composited onto a light-grey (#E8E8E8) website backdrop with a realistic soft contact shadow

Embossed leather front-centre badge selectively enhanced via a prompt-mask (local exposure lift + highlight shaping + dark-portion deepening + leather-hue nudge) so the emboss reads premium without looking AI-processed
Wicker selectively warmed and its yellow saturation lifted under a prompt-mask so only the basket warms, not the ribbons or food
One hero hamper with the fabric/material beneath it removed so the product sits clean on the backdrop
Two-stage editorial depth: whole-frame lens-blur on un-cut lifestyle frames + subject-preserving gaussian background blur on catalogue frames
Web-ready 2048×2048 Shopify square exports plus the saved, reusable edit recipe applied across the full set

workflow.note: Open an upload session for the raw 13-hamper set + the hero fabric-base frame.
workflow.consumes: input: 13 raw hamper JPGs + 1 hero fabric-base frame

workflow.note: Commit the uploads; returns fetchable presigned asset URLs used as imageURI for the whole chain.
Inline-preview each finalized upload to confirm the right frame/orientation before grading.
Level each handheld frame (basket lip / table-edge horizon).
Trim the straightened frame tight to the hamper bounds, killing dead studio floor.
Establish a neutral tonal baseline so every frame starts from the same place before manual grading.
Match global exposure to the agreed set key value (consistency across all 13).
Pull white balance to the warm quiet-luxury target; neutralise the mixed shoot lighting.
Recover blown bright wicker / lid highlights so texture survives.
Tone down the broader bright areas (lid, pale tissue) without crushing highlights — separate control from step 9.
Lift the shadows inside the basket weave to open editorial depth (negative amount = lift).
Set the gentle quiet-luxury contrast curve over the corrected tonal range.
Pull overall saturation back for restraint (vibrance protects food, saturation overall down).
Prompt-mask 'the woven wicker basket body of the hamper' — produces a wicker-only mask.
workflow.consumes: step13 graded image (mask computed on it)

workflow.note: MASKED warm-up: local temperature push to the wicker ONLY via step14 maskURI, ribbons/food stay neutral.
workflow.consumes: step13 graded image + step14 wicker mask

workflow.note: MASKED yellow lift: boost 'yellow' saturation under the wicker mask so the basket glows honey-toned, nothing else.
workflow.consumes: step15 wicker-warmed output + step14 wicker mask

workflow.note: Prompt-mask 'the embossed leather badge on the front centre of the hamper' — tight badge mask.
MASKED local lift on the badge only (step17 mask) so the emboss brightens without touching the rest.
MASKED highlight shaping on the badge (step17 mask) to catch the raised leather edges.
workflow.consumes: step18 badge-lifted output + step17 badge mask

workflow.note: MASKED deepen of the badge's recessed channels (step17 mask, positive amount) so the emboss reads 3-D.
MASKED hue/lum nudge on the badge leather (step17 mask) to land the rich tan without an orange cast.
List the 80+ Lightroom presets to pick the exact display name for the shared final look.
Apply ONE shared preset (e.g. a Creative warm split-tone) as the single, non-stacked final colour pass that locks the set look.
workflow.consumes: step21 fully-graded image + step22 chosen preset name

workflow.note: LIFESTYLE FRAMES ONLY: whole-frame lens blur for soft editorial bokeh (lens_blur has no mask = whole image).
CATALOGUE FRAMES: auto-detect the hamper as subject — subject mask reused for bg-blur and the cutout.
Subject-preserving background blur (blurTarget:'background') to settle the catalogue backdrop before the clean cut — keeps the hamper crisp.
workflow.consumes: step23 preset-locked frame (subject kept via blurTarget)

workflow.note: HERO FRAME: prompt-mask 'the draped fabric/material underneath the hamper' so it can be removed.
Fill the fabric mask (step27) with the light-grey backdrop colour (custom RGB 232,232,232) so the hero product sits clean — removes the base material.
workflow.consumes: step26 hero frame + step27 fabric mask

workflow.note: Flip the subject mask (step25) to a background mask for the studio backdrop fill.
Fill the inverted (background) mask with light-grey #E8E8E8 (RGB 232,232,232) — the clean Shopify studio backdrop behind the hamper.
workflow.consumes: step28 cleaned frame + step29 background mask

workflow.note: Produce a transparent cutout PNG of the hamper (clean edges) used to composite the realistic contact shadow locally.
Export each composited hamper to the Shopify 2048×2048 square at web resolution.
Preview the final 2048 square exports to QA the set for consistency before delivery.

LOCAL: drop the step31 transparent cutout onto the #E8E8E8 backdrop and paint a soft realistic contact shadow (Gaussian-feathered ellipse) under each hamper — the connector cuts/grades elements but can't headlessly composite a hand-painted shadow.

input_assets.what: Raw luxury hamper catalogue + lifestyle photos (mixed shoot lighting, wicker, embossed leather front badge) — the 13-image set
Hero hamper frame with draped fabric/material underneath that must be removed before compositing
Brand reference look-board (Fortnum & Mason / Daylesford quiet-luxury aesthetic) anchoring the target grade
Light-grey website background swatch + #E8E8E8 hex spec the cutouts must sit on
Set-grade recipe sheet (target exposure key, WB, shadow-lift, preset name) as a small JSON the editor follows for consistency
Shopify image-spec CSV (filename, SKU, target square px, max file weight) driving the export/QA pass

authored.why_creative_and_complex: A real quiet-luxury Shopify retouch brief expanded into a 34-step, 26-distinct-[C]-tool pipeline that is a near-pure stress test of the masking + tonal + preset + blur families requested. The chain is deeply iterative: a 7-stage global tonal grade (auto_tone→exposure→color_temp→highlights→light_portions→dark_portions→brightness_contrast→vibrance) feeds two prompt-mask sub-chains — a wicker mask driving masked color_temperature + masked single_color_saturation, and a badge mask driving four stacked MASKED edits (exposure, highlights, dark_portions, hsl) to sculpt the emboss in 3-D — then a single non-stacked shared preset locks the set look, after which two distinct depth treatments (whole-frame lens_blur for lifestyle, subject-preserving gaussian background blur for catalogue) and a full background-replacement recipe (select_subject→invert→fill_area at #E8E8E8, plus a separate fabric-removal fill on the hero) produce the clean studio cutout. Every step consumes the prior output or a mask derived from it. It exercises masked vs unmasked variants of the same tool, both blur tools used for their correct distinct purposes per the feasibility doc, and the full select/invert/fill cutout recipe. Only one honest [L] step remains (the hand-painted contact shadow the connector cannot headlessly composite); zero [X] tools; no authored templates needed.

authored.title: Real-estate weekly listing stills package — 6 scenes from 5-bracket RAW: masked window-pull HDR grade, straightened verticals, one shared gallery preset, print + web JPEGs, plus Illustrator data-merged 'just-listed' rider labels
authored.one_line_ask: Take 5 bracketed exposures per scene (6 scenes), blend to a natural-HDR look via fine tonal grading, straighten verticals / correct perspective, mask and recover blown windows (window-pull), open interiors while keeping clean neutral whites, calm over-green lawns / over-blue skies, lock one consistent gallery preset across all 6 for a cohesive set, export print-resolution + web/portal JPEGs of each, then data-merge a small batch of Illustrator 'just-listed' rider / yard-sign labels (address, beds/baths/sqft, price, agent) from the listing CSV using a user-authored .ai Variables template.
deliverables: 6 final natural-HDR scene stills with balanced exposure, recovered window highlights, opened shadows, clean neutral whites and corrected vertical perspective
Straight verticals / corrected geometry on every interior and exterior frame
Masked window-pull on each window-bearing interior so exterior detail reads instead of blowing out
A single shared gallery 'look' (one Lightroom preset) applied across all 6 so the set is cohesive
Print-ready high-resolution JPEG export of every scene (full-res, ~300dpi target)
Web/portal-optimized JPEG export of every scene (portal long-edge, sRGB)
A batch of Illustrator-merged 'just-listed' rider / yard-sign labels (address, beds/baths/sqft, price, agent name+phone) rendered to print PDF + PNG from the listing CSV
A local contact-sheet/gallery proof PDF laying out all 6 graded scenes for client sign-off
authored.source: Upwork db-6004 'Real Estate Photo Editor Needed for Bracketed RAW Interior & Exterior Images' (verbatim-anchored in pipeline/data/adobe.db items.id=6004): "each final image is normally captured using 5 RAW bracketed exposures ... Natural and realistic, not over-edited; Clean whites and balanced colors; Good window recovery when possible; Straight verticals and corrected perspective; Bright but still realistic interiors; Proper contrast and sharpness; Consistent look across the full gallery." Rider-label sub-deliverable is a standard real-estate listing-collateral add-on.
authored.why_creative_and_complex: A real bracketed-RAW real-estate gallery is a long, genuinely iterative grading pipeline, not a one-click filter: each of 6 scenes is straightened, perspective-trimmed, neutral-baselined, then walked through a deliberate tonal stack (exposure → masked highlight/window-pull → light-portion knock-down → dark-portion lift → contrast → neutral-white temperature → targeted HSL on lawns/skies → vibrance), and only then unified under ONE shared preset so the whole set looks shot together. The window-pull alone forces a real masking sub-chain (select_by_prompt on 'windows' → invert → masked highlight/exposure recovery). It then spans apps: Lightroom/Photoshop element grading → dual JPEG exports → Illustrator data-merge of variable rider labels from the listing CSV → vector render to print PDF, plus a local proof sheet. Most steps consume the previous step's output, and the masked window-pull plus the variable-data Illustrator labels make it both creative (look-locking) and structurally complex (multi-mask, multi-app, multi-format).

input_assets.what: 5-bracket exposures per scene across 6 scenes (interior living/kitchen/bed + exterior front + twilight + backyard); we generate a realistic merged-base JPEG per bracket set that the connector grades — documentary realism, true window blow-out in the base so the window-pull is load-bearing
input_assets.generator: gpt-image-2 (photoreal interiors/exteriors with genuinely blown windows + crooked verticals to fix)

input_assets.what: Agency 'house look' reference still (target natural-HDR gallery style) used to choose and lock the one shared preset
Listing fact-sheet CSV — one row per listing: address, beds, baths, sqft, price, status ('JUST LISTED'), agent_name, agent_phone, brokerage — drives the Illustrator rider labels
input_assets.generator: gemini-2.5-flash writer (CSV with header row matching the .ai Variable names exactly)

input_assets.what: User-authored Illustrator (.ai) 'just-listed' rider / yard-sign label template with genuine Illustrator Variables bound to text objects (NOT literal text) — supplied once by the client
Brokerage logo (.ai vector) to render onto the rider label artboard
input_assets.generator: gemini-3-pro-image NBP logo → vectorized, or client-supplied .ai

templates_to_author: rider_label.ai — Illustrator artboard 254mm x 152mm (10in x 6in yard-sign rider), 3mm bleed. Author in desktop Illustrator with the Variables panel; bind these as genuine Variables (Dynamic objects), NOT literal text: {{status}} (top banner text object, e.g. 'JUST LISTED'), {{address}} (headline text), {{beds}}, {{baths}}, {{sqft}} (three icon-paired stat text objects), {{price}} (large price text), {{agent_name}}, {{agent_phone}} (footer line), {{brokerage}} (footer). One linked-image Variable {{logo}} for the brokerage logo placeholder. CSV header row must read exactly: status,address,beds,baths,sqft,price,agent_name,agent_phone,brokerage,logo — column names matching the Variable names so document_merge_data_vector binds one artboard per row. Layout: red status banner top, bold address line, 3-up bed/bath/sqft stat row centered, price bottom-left, agent block bottom-right, logo top-right.

workflow.note: Open upload session for the 6 merged-base scene JPEGs (one representative blended frame per 5-bracket set) + the look-reference still.
workflow.consumes: input: 6 merged-base scene JPEGs + agency look reference (image inputs)

workflow.note: Finalize uploads; returns fetchable presigned URLs the editing tools chain from.
Per scene: level horizon and correct leaning verticals (the brief's 'straight verticals / corrected perspective'). Run once per scene; subsequent steps describe the per-scene chain.
Trim the crooked/empty edges introduced by the upright correction so the frame is clean.
Neutral exposure/contrast baseline before manual grading — gives a consistent starting point across all 6.
Set the mid exposure between the bracket extremes — bright but realistic interior (brief: 'Bright but still realistic interiors').
Mask the blown windows: select_by_prompt 'windows' to isolate the over-exposed glazing for the window-pull (brief: 'Good window recovery when possible').
workflow.consumes: step 6 exposed scene (produces a window mask)

workflow.note: Hold the window mask as-is for the masked recovery; invert is used where we need the room (non-window) region for the shadow lift in step 11. Establishes both polarities of the window mask.
Masked highlight recovery on the window region — pull blown windows down so exterior detail returns (the actual window-pull).
workflow.consumes: step 6 exposed scene + step 7 window mask

workflow.note: Tone down the brightest bright walls / ceiling hot-spots globally so interiors read evenly without clipping.
Lift dark corners / shadowed interiors using the inverted (room) mask region — open the shadows while leaving recovered windows intact.
Final overall punch and proper contrast (brief: 'Proper contrast and sharpness') without going over-edited.
Neutralize white balance for clean whites — kill mixed-light tungsten/daylight cast (brief: 'Clean whites and balanced colors').
Targeted HSL: calm over-green lawns and over-blue/cyan skies on exteriors; keep wood/wall tones natural.
Gentle vibrance lift for lively-but-realistic color across the set without oversaturating skin/wood.
Fetch exact Lightroom preset display names so the shared gallery look is applied by real name, not a guess.
workflow.consumes: agency look reference (step 2) informs which preset to pick

workflow.note: Apply ONE shared gallery preset (e.g. a subtle natural/clean profile) to every scene as the final unifying color pass — cohesive set (brief: 'Consistent look across the full gallery'). Applied once per scene with the same name.
workflow.consumes: step 15 graded scene + step 16 preset name

workflow.note: Export PRINT-resolution JPEG of each finished scene (full-res, listing print/flyer use).
Export WEB/portal JPEG of each scene (portal long-edge ~2048px, sRGB) — second derivative from the same master.
Assemble the 6 web JPEGs into a single client proof / contact-sheet PDF for sign-off (multi-element layout = local PIL/reportlab, per FEASIBILITY [L]).
Recommend a clean real-estate signage typeface pairing for the rider label (headline + stats) to inform the authored .ai template.
workflow.consumes: input: brand/look reference (drives label type choice)

workflow.note: Data-merge the listing CSV into the user-authored rider_label.ai (Illustrator Variables) — one artboard per listing row. Requires the genuine .ai Variables template (see templates_to_author); a literal-text .ai will NOT bind.
workflow.consumes: input: listing CSV + authored rider_label.ai; informed by step 21 font choice

workflow.note: Render the merged rider-label .ai artboards to print PDF (vector, bleed-preserving) for the sign shop.
Render the same merged rider labels to PNG at print DPI for quick digital proofs / web preview — second format from the merge output.
Bundle the final deliverable set (6 print JPEGs + 6 web JPEGs + rider-label PDF/PNGs + proof sheet) into the client delivery folder/index — local packaging.

authored.title: Reality-show key-art rebuild — repaint every AI region of an approved competition-show poster with masked, licensed Adobe Stock photography: each plate auto-toned, leveled, subject-cut, depth-blurred, cinematically re-graded with a shared preset and re-lit to one key, contact-shadowed, then composited, generative-expanded to 24x36 full-bleed, logo re-vectorized, and shipped as a genuine print-ready InDesign PDF plus a flattened web JPG
authored.one_line_ask: Take the network-approved AI key-art for a prime-time reality competition show (composition + typography locked) and rebuild it so it reads as photographed: source and license real Adobe Stock plates that match each AI region (two cast stand-ins, a dusk sky, a stadium-floor environment texture), normalize/level/cut/depth-blur and grade each plate to one shared cinematic look, re-light to a single warm key with matched exposure/highlights/shadows/temperature/HSL, drop believable contact shadows under the cut-outs, composite the plates, generatively expand the canvas to a full 24x36-inch bleed, re-vectorize the show logo lockup crisp for print, and deliver a genuine print-ready InDesign PDF (300dpi) plus a flattened web JPG with a stock-usage note.
deliverables: Licensed Adobe Stock photography matching every AI region of the approved poster — two cast stand-ins (hero + foil), a dusk-gradient sky plate, and a stadium-floor/arena environment texture — with a stock-usage note listing every asset id and license
Each plate individually normalized (auto-tone), leveled (auto-straighten + crop-to-bounds), and subject-cut to a clean alpha cut-out that drops over its corresponding AI region

Background sky + environment plates depth-blurred (background-target gaussian on the plate that retains a subject; whole-plate lens-blur on the standalone sky) so foreground cast pop and the set falls off naturally

Every merged layer re-lit to ONE warm key with matched exposure, highlights, dark/light portions, brightness/contrast, colour temperature and per-channel HSL — plus a shared Lightroom cinematic preset across all plates so the scene reads as a single lit set with no AI artifacts at 200% zoom
Believable contact/cast shadows filled under each cut-out and a warm key-light colour wash married across the composite
The show's title + billing-block logo lockup re-vectorized crisp and rendered to a print-resolution vector proof, with a recommended billing-block typeface matched to the locked typography
Canvas generative-expanded from the approved crop to a full 24x36-inch (300dpi) bleed
Final deliverables: a genuine print-ready InDesign PDF at 24x36 / 300dpi and a flattened web JPG

workflow.note: Open an upload session and push the client-supplied inputs into Adobe storage: the approved AI key-art (flattened JPG + layered PSD reference, typography/comp locked), the print spec sheet, and the mood/lighting reference frame.
workflow.consumes: input: approved AI poster JPG+PSD, print spec, mood-ref frame (client assets)

workflow.note: Finalize the upload session; receive stable asset handles/URLs for the approved poster and references used as the visual target throughout.
Search Adobe Stock (entityScope StockAsset; filter orientation=vertical, contentType=photo) for a HERO cast stand-in whose pose/wardrobe/lighting matches the approved poster's lead figure.
workflow.consumes: step2 finalized poster as the matching brief -> Stock query

workflow.note: License + download the chosen hero stand-in at full resolution.
workflow.consumes: step3 (StockAsset id, hero) -> full-res hero plate

workflow.note: Search Stock again for the FOIL/second cast stand-in plus a dusk-gradient SKY plate and a stadium-floor/arena ENVIRONMENT-texture plate that match the AI background regions.
workflow.consumes: step2 poster regions -> Stock queries (foil, sky, environment)

workflow.note: License + download the foil stand-in, the sky plate, and the environment-texture plate at full resolution (3 more plates).
Normalize each freshly licensed plate to a neutral baseline so later grade math starts from a consistent point (run per plate: hero, foil, sky, environment).
Level the environment/arena plate so the floor and horizon are true before it is cut and composited.
Crop the rotation slack off the straightened environment plate, locking clean edges for the composite.
workflow.consumes: step8 straightened environment -> tightly bounded environment plate

workflow.note: Auto-select the HERO stand-in figure to build its cut-out mask.
workflow.consumes: step7 hero plate -> hero subject mask

workflow.note: Use the hero selection to strip the background, yielding a clean alpha cut-out that will drop over the AI hero region.
workflow.consumes: step10 hero mask -> hero alpha cut-out

workflow.note: Auto-select the FOIL stand-in figure on its plate.
workflow.consumes: step7 foil plate -> foil subject mask

workflow.note: Strip the foil plate background to a second clean alpha cut-out.
workflow.consumes: step12 foil mask -> foil alpha cut-out

workflow.note: On the environment plate, prompt-select 'the sky' so the upper region can be isolated from the arena floor for separate depth treatment.
Invert the sky mask to select the foreground arena-floor region of the environment plate (the part that must stay sharper).
workflow.consumes: step14 sky mask -> inverted floor-region mask

workflow.note: Apply background-target gaussian blur to the environment plate (blurTarget:background) so the deep arena recedes while the floor under the cast stays readable.
workflow.consumes: step15 floor mask + step9 environment -> depth-blurred environment plate

workflow.note: Apply whole-plate lens blur to the standalone dusk SKY plate (lens_blur has no mask) to render a soft, optically believable far-distance bokeh fall-off behind everything.
workflow.consumes: step7 sky plate -> lens-blurred sky backdrop

workflow.note: Match overall exposure of the blurred environment plate to the approved poster's mood (anchor the grade chain on the largest background plate).
Pull/lift highlights to seat the rim/key-light response consistent with the poster's specular look.
Refine the upper-midtone/light portions so the lit areas of the set carry the same falloff as the reference.
Deepen the dark portions to seat believable shadow density beneath where the cast cut-outs will sit.
Set global contrast/brightness so the plate's tonal range matches the poster's punchy key-art look.
Unify warm/cool balance toward the single warm key the poster is lit with.
Calm overall vibrance/saturation so the licensed photography doesn't read more saturated than the approved comp.
Rein in the one over-saturated channel (the dusk-orange sky band) so it sits believably against the floor instead of glowing.
Final per-channel HSL trim to lock exact hue/sat/luminance match between the background plate and the approved poster palette.
List available Lightroom presets to choose one shared cinematic look (e.g. an Adaptive Subject-Pop / cinematic split-tone) to apply across ALL plates for a single coherent grade.
workflow.consumes: step26 graded background as the look reference -> preset shortlist

workflow.note: Apply the chosen shared cinematic preset to the hero cut-out, foil cut-out, sky backdrop and background plate so every layer carries identical tonal DNA (no AI-look mismatch).
workflow.consumes: step27 chosen preset + step11/step13/step17/step26 plates -> uniformly graded plate set

workflow.note: Wash a warm key-light colour overlay across the graded plates to marry them under one lighting colour, selling the 'one lit set' read.
workflow.consumes: step28 graded plate set -> key-light-married plates

workflow.note: On a transparent shadow layer, select the ground contact zone beneath each cast cut-out and fill it with a soft dark tone (custom RGB) to create believable contact/cast shadows anchoring the figures.
workflow.consumes: step11+step13 cut-outs over step29 floor -> contact-shadow shadow plate

workflow.note: Recommend a billing-block / title typeface matched to the locked typography so the re-set logo lockup and credit block are typographically faithful.
workflow.consumes: step2 poster typography reference -> recommended billing-block font

workflow.note: Vectorize the show's title + billing-block logo lockup from the approved poster into crisp scalable artwork (SVG/.ai) for print, replacing the soft AI raster logo.
workflow.consumes: step2 approved poster logo region -> vector logo lockup (.ai/SVG)

workflow.note: Render the vectorized logo lockup .ai to a print-resolution proof (PNG/PDF) for placement in the final layout.
workflow.consumes: step32 vector logo .ai -> rendered print-res logo proof

workflow.note: Generative-expand the composed key-art's short axis out to a full 24x36-inch bleed so there is real image data into the trim/bleed margin.

authored.why_creative_and_complex: A genuine, well-defined single deliverable (rebuild ONE approved poster), not a job ad — yet it forces the full stock-composite key-art pipeline across Stock + Photoshop + Lightroom + Illustrator + InDesign + local compose. It is creative because every AI region must be re-sourced from real licensed photography and made to read as a single lit set: four separately-licensed plates each get their own normalize/level/cut/depth pipeline, then ALL are unified by one shared cinematic preset and a matched 10-step relight (exposure -> highlights -> light/dark portions -> brightness/contrast -> temperature -> vibrance -> single-colour -> HSL -> colour-overlay key wash -> contact-shadow fill). It is complex and long-horizon (38 steps, 25 distinct connector-confirmed tools) with a tightly iterative chain: most steps consume the previous step's output, the four plate pipelines converge into one composite, the composite is generative-expanded to 24x36 bleed, the logo is re-vectorized and proofed, and the whole thing lands as a genuine InDesign print PDF plus a flattened web JPG. The lens_blur-vs-gaussian masking distinction, the standalone-sky-vs-subject-plate handling, and the local-only final multi-element compose are all honored exactly per the confirmed feasibility rules.

authored.why_creative_and_complex: This is the flagship DATA-MERGE task and the only one in the suite that exercises both InDesign merge tools end-to-end as real connector steps now that CC Pro entitles InDesign. It is genuinely long-horizon (26 steps, 19 distinct [C] connector-confirmed tools) and almost fully iterative: a single raw headshot is chained through 13 consecutive output→input image edits (straighten → face-extract crop → auto-tone → exposure → highlights → brightness/contrast → colour-temp → HSL → select-subject → invert → fill-area backdrop → list-presets → apply-preset) to manufacture one uniform retouched portrait, repeated across the batch, and that retouched set is then injected into a genuine InDesign data merge via additionalImageFiles. Two desktop-authored templates (badge.indd + certificate.indd) are both driven from ONE roster CSV as the single source of truth, producing 1,020 print-ready CMYK 300dpi documents across two deliverables. It spans Lightroom/Photoshop (retouch + vectorize), Type (font_recommend), and InDesign (convert_pdf + merge_data_layout x2 + render_layout proofs and full export), with correct mask polarity (subject→invert→fill), names-before-apply (list_presets→apply_preset), and a proof-then-full-run gate. The creative judgment — manufacturing visual uniformity across 510 wildly inconsistent attendee snapshots so a print run looks like one cohesive set — is exactly the kind of real, well-defined freelance brief the benchmark targets, and it covers data-merge as a connector that no other task in the suite reaches headlessly."

workflow.note: Open upload sessions for all client inputs: 6 room walkthrough clips, the rough agent VO+music audio, the 4 listing stills (hero interior, exterior, twilight reference, detail).
Finalize the uploads; obtain stable asset handles/URLs for every clip, the audio, and the stills used by all downstream steps.

Find the key spaces and spoken beats across the 6 room clips to decide room order and the 3-second hook. Connector entitled but async-unretrievable headless -> run locally (ffmpeg scene-detect + whisper-style transcript). [L-or-connector] default local.

Isolate + de-reverb the agent narration from its rough music/ambient bed into a clean dialogue stem. Connector entitled but async-unretrievable headless -> run locally (ffmpeg/RNNoise-class denoise + de-reverb). [L-or-connector] default local.
Adobe Stock: search a royalty-free music bed AND a twilight/dusk sky plate (entityScope StockAsset; filter contentType audio for the track, image/orientation landscape for the sky).
License + download the chosen music bed and twilight sky plate to full-res/licensed files for the mix and the exterior composite.

Assemble the tight tour: order rooms per the step-3 summary, hook in first 3s, trim/concat the strongest room segments. Connector async-unretrievable headless -> ffmpeg cut/concat. [L-or-connector] default local.

Mux the step-4 cleaned VO + step-6 licensed music under the cut, then reframe/export the tour to Feed 1:1, Stories 9:16, Reels 9:16. Connector async-unretrievable headless -> ffmpeg reframe/scale/mux. [L-or-connector] default local.

workflow.note: Level the hero interior still (correct the tilted phone horizon/verticals) before grading.
Trim the auto-straighten transparent border so the levelled frame is edge-to-edge.
Establish a neutral tonal baseline on the cropped hero before manual grading.
Neutralize mixed interior white balance (warm tungsten vs cool window light) for the campaign look.
Brighten the interior to the bright-airy listing standard.
Mask the windows ('windows') to recover the blown exterior view selectively.
On the window mask, pull highlights to recover blown window detail (masked edit).
Invert the window mask to address the room interior (everything but the windows).
On the inverted (interior) mask, lift dark portions to open up shadowed corners.
Balance the light portions of the interior so walls/ceiling read clean and even.
HSL tune greens (lawn/plants through windows) and blues (sky) toward the campaign palette.
Add vibrance for feed pop without oversaturating skin/wood tones.
List Lightroom presets to pick the shared campaign 'listing look' (e.g. an Adaptive/Creative real-estate-friendly preset).
Apply the chosen preset to lock the consistent listing aesthetic on the hero.
Generative-expand the graded hero to fill a 9:16 canvas (and a 1:1 canvas) without cropping the room — clean reframe for Stories/Reels and Feed covers.
Cut the per-platform cover stills + the video thumbnail from the expanded hero: Feed 1:1, Stories/Reels 9:16, and a thumbnail crop.
On the exterior still, mask the existing flat/overcast sky ('sky') to prep the twilight swap.
Fill the masked sky region as a clean plate (solid) to seat the licensed twilight plate behind the building in local compositing; connector handles the masked element prep.

workflow.note: Cut the brokerage logo/agent badge off its background for the lower-third / logo bug.
Vectorize the cut-out logo to a clean scalable SVG for the lower-third bug overlaid on every export.
Recommend listing typography for the lower-third agent name + price strip (legible at Reels scale).
Inline-preview the graded covers, twilight composite, vector bug, and resized exports for the client approval contact sheet before delivery.

Local assembly: composite the twilight plate behind the masked exterior, lay the vector logo + typed lower-third (agent name/price) onto each cover and the video thumbnail, and pack the delivery contact sheet. Multi-element layout is local per FEASIBILITY.

workflow.note: Initialize upload of the approved inked hero illustration (full-bleed cover composition, ~4500px) into Adobe storage to seed the whole pipeline.
workflow.consumes: input asset: approved inked cover illustration PNG (~4500px)

workflow.note: Finalize the upload; returns the working presigned URL that every downstream cover-treatment step reads from.
Normalize the scanned/inked artwork's overall tone so blacks read true and the colour base is even before any variant treatments diverge — establishes the master raster all variants fork from.
Crop/resize the toned master to the exact comic trim-with-bleed canvas (6.625x10.25in @300dpi, full-bleed) so every variant shares identical dimensions and registration.
Select the central VOIDRUNNER figure (hero subject) to isolate it for figure-specific grading separate from the background sky/cityscape.
Push vibrance/saturation on the masked figure only (using the step-5 subject mask) to make the hero pop for the Cover A standard treatment.
Invert the subject selection to target the background (sky + neon city) for separate atmospheric grading.
Cool the inverted background mask toward signal-cyan/blue night-city tones, deepening the sci-fi mood behind the warm-graded figure — completes the Cover A colour grade.
Crush the dark portions on the full Cover A frame to restore deep inked blacks after grading; this is the finished Cover A colour master.
Fork the Cover B 'Retro' variant: apply a Ben-Day-style halftone dot screen over the Cover A master for the retro-print look.
Flatten the halftoned Retro variant toward a limited 3-colour Ben-Day palette by collapsing hue/sat ranges (reds/cyans/yellows) — finishes Cover B.
Fork the Cover C 'Noir' variant: apply a two-colour duotone tint (ink-black shadows -> signal-cyan highlights) to the Cover A master.
Crank contrast on the duotone to harden the noir light/shadow separation — finishes Cover C.
Fork the Cover D 'Cyber' 1:10 incentive variant: apply RGB-split VHS glitch distortion to the Cover A master for the cyberpunk treatment.
Layer analog film grain over the glitched variant to sell the degraded-broadcast aesthetic — finishes Cover D.
Search Adobe Stock for a high-res grunge/scratched-metal distress texture for the Cover E 'Battle-Damage' 1:25 incentive variant.
workflow.consumes: new input: Adobe Stock query (grunge distress texture)

workflow.note: License and download the chosen grunge distress texture at full resolution to use as the Battle-Damage overlay source.
Begin Cover E: apply a scorched amber/oxblood colour overlay to the Cover A master to base-tint the battle-damage variant before the distress texture goes on.

Add coarse noise to the colour-overlaid Battle-Damage base so the licensed grunge texture reads as scratched/abraded ink — finishes the Cover E look (texture from step 17 composited locally at layout).

Fork the Cover F 'Sketch' retailer-exclusive: vectorize the auto-toned inked master into clean black-and-white line-art (no fills) -> SVG, the deliverable sketch variant and the reusable line-art base.
Initialize upload of the separately-supplied VOIDRUNNER title/logo lockup raster so it can be vectorized into a crisp reusable SVG.
workflow.consumes: input asset: VOIDRUNNER logo/title lockup PNG

workflow.note: Finalize the logo upload; returns the URL for vectorization.
Vectorize the title/logo lockup to a clean SVG that scales identically across all six covers' trade dress.
Recommend a credit-line / issue-number typeface pairing (display + secondary) that matches the gritty sci-fi logo for the trade-dress block.
For the Retro spot-separation sheet, generate the halftone dot field that the underbase + spot plates will be built from (the Retro variant's screen pattern, isolated).

Drive the Illustrator trade-dress data merge: bind the vectorized logo (step 23), the recommended type (step 24), and each variant's trade-dress fields from a 6-row CSV (one row per cover A-F) to produce one composed cover-trade-dress artboard per variant — issue #, colourway name, variant ratio, cover price, barcode zone. Needs the authored .ai with Variables.
workflow.consumes: variant rasters (steps 9,11,13,15,19,20) + step 23 (logo SVG) + step 24 (type) + trade-dress CSV

workflow.note: Render the merged Illustrator trade-dress artboards (all six variants) to print-ready PDFs and 2048px web JPGs — the primary cover deliverables.

Render the authored 4-colour spot-separation/registration sheet .ai (underbase + 3 spot plates carrying the step-25 halftone dot field, with corner registration marks) to a print PDF — the press-ready separation deliverable for the Retro variant.
workflow.consumes: separation .ai (driven by spot-colour CSV) + step 25 (halftone dot field)

templates_to_author: Illustrator trade-dress template 'voidrunner_variant_covers.ai' (authored once in desktop Illustrator, Variables panel bound). One artboard family sized to comic trim+bleed (6.625x10.25in, 0.125in bleed all sides, 300dpi). Layers: (1) ART layer = a linked/placed-image frame that receives each variant raster (the data-merge swaps the placed graphic per row — Variable {cover_art} bound to the linked file); (2) MASTHEAD layer = the vectorized VOIDRUNNER logo SVG (static, identical every cover); (3) TRADE-DRESS layer = text frames bound to Variables {issue_no}, {colourway_name}, {variant_ratio}, {cover_price}, {credit_line}, {variant_code}, plus a reserved white UPC-barcode rectangle in the lower-left whose visibility is bound to Variable {barcode_zone} (on for newsstand, off for direct-market). CSV column headers MUST match the Variable names EXACTLY: cover_art, issue_no, colourway_name, variant_ratio, cover_price, credit_line, variant_code, barcode_zone. 6 rows, one per cover: A 'Standard'/1:1/$4.99/barcode on; B 'Retro'/1:1/$4.99/barcode on; C 'Noir'/1:1/$4.99/barcode on; D 'Cyber'/1:10/$9.99/barcode off; E 'Battle-Damage'/1:25/$19.99/barcode off; F 'Sketch'/retailer-exclusive/$14.99/barcode off.

Illustrator spot-separation template 'voidrunner_retro_sep_sheet.ai' (authored once in desktop Illustrator, Variables panel bound). One MASTER SEPARATION artboard at trim+bleed: four labelled plate-placeholder frames stacked (WHITE UNDERBASE / SPOT 1 / SPOT 2 / SPOT 3), each receiving the Retro variant's isolated halftone dot field; corner crop/registration target marks at all four corners + a centre bullseye; a title block bound to Variables {job_name}, {issue_no}, {total_screens}, {lpi}, {date}. Plus per-ink swatch cards (artboards 2..5, one per separation CSV row): a 3.5x2in card whose swatch rectangle fill is driven by Variable {rgb_hex} and text frames bound to {ink_name}, {pantone}, {print_order}, {lpi}. Separation CSV headers MUST match exactly: ink_name, pantone, rgb_hex, print_order, lpi. 4 rows: 'White Underbase'/no Pantone/FFFFFF/order 1; 'Ben-Day Red'/185C/ED1C24/order 2; 'Ben-Day Cyan'/2995C/00AEEF/order 3; 'Ink Black'/Black 6C/231F20/order 4.
authored.domain: Comic / graphic-novel variant-cover production (Illustrator vector + Photoshop/Lightroom FX + Adobe Stock + data-merge)
authored.provenance: Designed from the FEASIBILITY.md [C]/[T] connector set + GROUNDING.md archetype E (creative-FX) crossed with archetype G (Illustrator vector data-merge); grounded in the real freelance pattern 'comic cover + variant colourways' (creator-owned/indie variant-cover commissions). Distinct from the existing mega_benchmark tech-house single (E/G) and 4-colour screen-print separation tasks: this one is Illustrator-trade-dress + per-variant FX-fork centric with a 6-treatment cover line.
authored.source_url: freelance pattern (Upwork/PeoplePerHour comic-cover + variant-colourway commissions) — no single canonical listing; archetype-grounded per GROUNDING.md
authored.why_creative_and_complex: A real variant-cover pack is intrinsically a long iterative fork-and-treat pipeline: one approved hero illustration must yield six commercially-distinct covers that all share registration, masthead and trade dress yet diverge into genuinely different print treatments (full-colour, Ben-Day halftone, duotone noir, glitch+grain cyber, licensed-grunge battle-damage, vectorized sketch). It legitimately spans four Adobe surfaces — Photoshop/Lightroom for the per-variant FX forks and masked grading, Adobe Stock for the licensed distress texture, Illustrator for the vectorized line-art/logo plus the Variables-driven trade-dress merge and the print render, and a spot-colour separation sheet for the retro variant's press run. The chain is deeply iterative: a single auto-toned/cropped/graded master (steps 3-9) is the trunk that every FX variant forks from, the cleanest pre-grade master feeds the vectorize, and the merge consumes all six finished variant rasters plus the vectorized logo and recommended type. It maximizes distinct creative-FX connectors (halftone, monochromatic_tint, glitch, grain, noise, color_overlay) alongside the masking pipeline (select_subject/invert) and the vector data-merge, with wide input variety (inked art + logo + stock texture + two CSVs + a spec JSON).
